title: Failure Containment Design

content.statement: Bad events are inevitable; systemic spread is optional.
content.question: If your business's payment processor failed tonight, how far would the damage spread? What's your containment plan?
content.equation: FCD = (Isolation × Detection × Recovery × Override) ÷ Cascade Potential
sop: List your top 3 critical systems (payments, hosting, communication, fulfillment) and map what else breaks if each one fails.
For each, write down how you'd detect the failure, ideally in minutes, not from a customer complaint.
Build one firebreak per critical system, a manual backup, a second vendor, an offline process, so failure doesn't cascade.
Rate Isolation, Detection, Recovery, and Override 1-10, and rate Cascade Potential 1-10 (see calculation, page 5)
Run a tabletop drill twice a year: system X is down right now, what do we do?
content.explainer: You can't prevent all failures. You can prevent them from cascading. Design firebreaks.
questions: If your business's payment processor failed tonight, how far would the damage spread? What's your containment plan?
Which single failure in your business would take down more than one system if it happened right now?
How long would it take you to even notice if your most critical system went down?
content.equation_example: Rate Isolation, Detection, Recovery, and Override 1-10 each, then rate Cascade Potential 1-10. Isolation 7, Detection 8, Recovery 6, Override 5, Cascade Potential 3 → FCD = (7×8×6×5)/3 = 560. Higher FCD means failures stay contained and recover fast; lower means one break threatens to take down everything connected to it.

caption:
Principle Nº 30: Failure Containment Design

Bad events are inevitable; systemic spread is optional. You can't prevent all failures.

Ask yourself: If your business's payment processor failed tonight, how far would the damage spread? What's your containment plan?
